rulings.Case Title: ALLAN QUITON VS. BRIAN MCWILLIAMS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Motion To Enforce Settlement Per Ccp 664.6
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, APRIL 8, 2019, LINE 2 PLAINTIFF ALLAN QUITON'S Motion To Enforce Settlement Per Ccp 664.6 DENIED. The Settlement Agreement of January 14, 2019 does not contain any language binding the parties to enter into a mutual release of claims. The waiver of Civil Code section 1542 in the January 14, 2019 Settlement Agreement is only applicable to the extent there is a general release of claims; it does not constitute a release on its own. The plain language of the Settlement Agreement in Exhibit A only releases Plaintiff's claims, by way of a dismissal with prejudice. It does not provide for a general waiver of any claims Defendants may have against Plaintiff. Furthermore, there is no objective manifestation of an agreement to mutually release claims as required for such a term to be part of an enforceable contract. (See Bowers v. Raymond J. Lucia Companies, Inc. (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th 724, 732-33 ["The existence of mutual consent is determined by objective rather than subjective criteria."].) A settlement is only enforceable with respect to material terms agreed to by all parties. "The court ruling on the motion [to enforce settlement] may consider the parties' declarations and other evidence in deciding what terms the parties agreed to. . . ." (Hines v. Lukes (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1174, 1182.) Here, Defendant Brian McWilliams directly contradicts Plaintiff's assertion that there was an agreement to mutually release claims. (McWilliams Decl. 5.) The only bargained-for exchange-and therefore the only agreement supported by consideration-is the release of Plaintiff's claims related to the current dispute in return for $20,000. (Nelson Decl. Exh. A; Civ. Code §1550.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, APRIL 8, 2019, LINE 3, Motion 6 of 6 CROSS-DEFENDANT RELIANT PROJECT MANAGEMENT, LLC'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT Cross-defendant Reliant Project Management, LLC's demurrer to Patrick McNerney's cross-complaint and to the first amended cross-complaint of Cross-Complainants Fifth Historic Properties, LLC, Martin McNerney Development, Inc., 418 Jessie Historic Properties, LLC and 418 Jessie Properties, LLC is overruled as to the second, third, and fifth causes of action for equitable indemnity, contribution, and negligence, and is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the first and fourth causes of action for contractual indemnity and breach of contract. The first amended cross-complaint sufficiently pleads causes of action for negligence, equitable indemnity, and contribution by alleging that Reliant owed a duty of care to cross-complainants in performing the repair and maintenance work, breached that duty by doing defective work (Cross-Complaint 49, 51, 59, 61, 65, 68) and shares in fault for damages sustained by plaintiff Mint Collection. (Hahn v. Mirda (2007) 147 Cal.App.4th 740, 747 ["negligence may be alleged in general terms; that is, it is sufficient to allege an act was negligently done without stating the particular omission which rendered it negligent."].) The demurrer to the contract-based causes of action is sustained pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure § 430.10(g) because it cannot be ascertained from the cross-complaint whether the contract(s) was written or oral (Cross-Complaint 63), and the pleading is also uncertain as to whether the breach of contract and contractual indemnity causes of action are based on one or two agreements. Cross-complainants are given leave to amend to cure these deficiencies. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Reliant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW BAILEY VS. CHINATOWN COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT CENTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Required Consent For Deposition Of Treating Psychiatrists
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, April 8, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT CHINATOWN COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT CENTER's Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Required Consent For Deposition Of Treating Psychiatrists. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Movants do not cite authority supporting the assertion that the Court has authority to order Plaintiff to execute a medical release. The Court has not independently found mandatory California authority, or persuasive non-California authority, permitting it to issue such an order. The appropriate procedure for obtaining the testimony likely would have been a motion to compel the recipients of the subpoenas to provide testimony. CCP 1985.3, 1987.1; see also correspondence from the City Attorney's Office (Kozlow Decl. Ex. L). In ruling on such a motion the Court could have imposed any necessary limitations to protect Plaintiff's privacy interests. Id. Therefore, the motion is DENIED. However, because Plaintiff listed Dr. Lee and Dr. Morrison as non-retained expert witnesses, failure to provide the executed release may serve to bar Plaintiff from calling Dr. Lee and/or Dr. Morrison at trial because Plaintiff has failed effectively to make them available for deposition. CCP 2034.300, 2034.410. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHARU SAROJ DAVE ET AL VS. 2121 LAGUNA STREET CONDOMINIUM HOMEOWNERS'S ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Further Responses To Defendants Requests For Production Of Documents Set Three And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, April 8, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT 2121 LAGUNA STREET CONDOMINIUM HOMEOWNERS'S, RAND RICHARDS' Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Further Responses To Defendants Requests For Production Of Documents Set Three And Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel is GRANTED IN PART: Siler and Gallagher (but not "persons acting on" their behalf) shall produce non-privileged communications that were exchanged between Siler and Gallagher, during the date range of March 2015 through February 28, 2017, and that relate to the condition of the building or to the HOA. Any materials previously produced (by any party) need not be re-produced. Any materials withheld because of a claim of privilege must be identified on a privilege log. This production shall occur on or before April 19, 2019. The basis for this order is that both Siler and Gallagher were on the HOA board during this period and may have materials related to Defendants' defense that the prior HOA board did not properly act to maintain the building. The motion is otherwise DENIED. The motion does not demonstrate how communications not related to the condition of the building or to the HOA are relevant to, or reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence relevant to, the claims or defenses of any party. Moreover, except as limited in this Order, the request would be overbroad and would appear to require production of otherwise private communications. The requests for sanctions by each side are DENIED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GUILLERMA BUENSALIDO VS. NFG SAN FRANCISCO LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Part 2, Plaintiff Guillerma Buensalido'S Motion For Judicial Approval Of Paga Settlement
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, APRIL 8, 2019, LINE 7, Part 2 of 2 Plaintiff Guillerma Buensalido'S Motion For Judicial Approval Of Paga Settlement Even if the Thirteenth Cause of Action were solely an individual claim, the claim and the motion are ill-conceived. There is no such thing as an "individual" PAGA claim. The Private Attorneys General Act of 2004 authorizes an aggrieved employee, acting as a private attorney general, to recover civil penalties for Labor Code violations "on behalf of himself or herself and other current or former employees against whom one or more of the alleged violations was committed." (Lab. Code § 2699(g)(1) (emphasis added).) A PAGA action "is a representative action on behalf of the state." (Iskanian v. CLS Transp. Los Angeles, LLC (2014) 59 Cal.4th 348, 387; see also Arias v. Superior Court (2009) 46 Cal.4th 969, 986.) "A plaintiff asserting a PAGA claim may not bring the claim simply on his or her own behalf but must bring it as a representative action and include 'other current or former employees.'" (Reyes v. Macy's, Inc. (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 1119, 1123; see also Kahn v. Dunn-Edwards Corp. (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 804, 810 n.1 ["[A] PAGA action is only a representative action. 'Permitting pursuit of only individual penalties appears inconsistent with PAGA's objectives.'"] (quoting Tanguilig v. Bloomingdale's, Inc. (2016) 5 Cal.App.5th 665, 676.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: GUILLERMA BUENSALIDO VS. NFG SAN FRANCISCO LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judicial Approval Of Paga Settlement
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, APRIL 8, 2019, LINE 7, Part 1 of 2 PLAINTIFF GUILLERMA BUENSALIDO'S Motion For Judicial Approval Of PAGA Settlement DENIED. Plaintiff asserts, "This is not a representative case. Instead, plaintiff, on behalf of herself alone presented a PAGA cause of action" for wrongful termination. (Memo. at 1; see also id. at 5 ["the complaint in this matter is limited to plaintiff's individual claim. . . . plaintiff simply does not allege representative claims, nor does she seek penalties on behalf of other employees."]; First Am. Compl. 181 ["Plaintiff brings this cause of action on behalf of herself . . ."].) Plaintiff's characterization of her thirteenth cause of action, captioned "Wrongful Discharge in Violation of Public Policy; Violation of the Private Attorneys General Act," as solely an individual claim appears to be inaccurate. In that cause of action, Plaintiff alleges that during the time period of her employment, from May 12, 2016 until August 19, 2017, defendants "failed to provide Plaintiff and, plaintiff is informed and believes, other Managers, Assistant Managers, Drivers, Delivery Experts, Cooks, and Waiters," with adequate off-duty meal and rest periods and failed to pay compensation owed to such employees for missed meal and rest periods. (First Am. Compl. 182-184.) She alleges further that after she complained to her supervisor that defendants' failure to provide her and "other employees" with required meal and rest periods was "against the law," her employment was terminated in retaliation for that complaint. (Id. 187, 191-192.) She also alleges that she gave notice to the California Labor and Workforce Development Agency of those alleged violations. (Id. 186-187.) That notice, attached as Exhibit A to the Pasquali Declaration, states that Plaintiff "provides notice on behalf of herself and of all individuals currently and formerly employed in California as hourly employees [by Defendants]." Among the alleged Labor Code violations listed in that notice is Labor Code § 1102.5 - the same provision cited in the Thirteenth Cause of Action. (First Am. Compl. 189.) Continued in Part 2 of 2....

rulings.Case Title: J NILEY DORIT VS. JACK NOE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Part 2, Defendant'S Motion For Order Denying Plaintiff'S Motion For Protective Order Filed August 17, 2018
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, APRIL 8, 2019, LINE 12, Par 2 of 2 In Brennan, the Court held that "[a] termination by contractual arbitration is simply not the sort of favorable termination needed to support a malicious prosecution action." (25 Cal.4th at 317.) The Court based its holding on (1) the trend against creating or expanding derivative tort remedies; and (2) the trend in favor of allowing parties voluntarily to choose binding, private arbitration to end their dispute. (Id. at 314.) Both factors apply to this case. First, the Court "cautioned against creating or expanding derivative tort remedies" in order to avoid giving rise to endless rounds of litigation; for those reasons, the Court noted, the tort of malicious prosecution, which is often described as disfavored, " 'should not be expanded.'" (Id.) Second, the Court noted the "increasing awareness that parties to litigation should be permitted to choose private arbitration as a means of resolving their dispute once and for all." (Id. at 315.) These factors, taken together, convinced the Court that "private arbitration should not be the basis for future malicious prosecution actions." (Id. at 315.) The court denies Plaintiff's request for leave to amend. The Court construes the complaint as pleading a single cause of action for malicious prosecution and that claim fails as a matter of law. Unlike Nguyen-Lam v. Cao (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 858, where the court allowed an amendment to conform to proof, Plaintiff cannot amend the complaint to cure the defective malicious prosecution claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = EPS

rulings.Case Title: J NILEY DORIT VS. JACK NOE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Carlos Del Olmo'S Motion For Order Denying Plaintiff'S Motion For Protective Order Filed August 17, 2018
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON MONDAY, APRIL 8, 2019, LINE 12, Part 1 of 2 Defendant Carlos Del Olmo'S Motion For Order Denying Plaintiff'S Motion For Protective Order Filed August 17, 2018 Defendant Jack Noe's anti-SLAPP motion to strike is granted. Defendant meets prong one of the anti-SLAPP statute. Plaintiff J. Niley Dorit's claim for malicious prosecution is based on Defendant's commencement of arbitration proceedings pursuant to the Mandatory Fee Arbitration Act, Bus. & Prof. Code § 6200 et seq. That arbitration is an "official proceeding" within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure § 425.16(e)(2). The text of the award makes it clear that the non-binding arbitration was initiated and conducted pursuant to the statutory scheme. "[T]he initiation of a State Bar sponsored fee arbitration proceeding . . . is an official proceeding established by statute to address a particular type of dispute." (Philipson & Simon v. Gulsvig (2007) 154 Cal.App.4th 347, 358.) The fact that the arbitration occurred before the Bar Association of San Francisco rather than the "State Bar" makes no difference to the analysis. The arbitration in Philipson arose from a fee arbitration before the Orange County Bar Association (Philipson, 154 Cal.App.4th at 353), and the MFAA expressly contemplates local bar association involvement. (Bus. & Prof. Code § 6200(d) ["The board of trustees shall adopt rules to allow arbitration and mediation of attorney fee and costs disputes under this article to proceed under arbitration and mediation systems sponsored by local bar associations in this state."]; Schatz v. Allen Matkins Leck Gamble & Mallory LLP (2009) 45 Cal.4th 557, 561 ["Under the mandatory fee arbitration act [citation], when there is a fee dispute between an attorney and a client, the client may choose to submit the matter to arbitration by a local bar association."].) Because Defendant met prong one of the anti-SLAPP statute, Plaintiff must show a likelihood of prevailing on his claim for malicious prosecution. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 425.16(b)(1).) Plaintiff cannot make that showing as a matter of law because the fee arbitration is not the type of "prior action" that can result in a favorable termination for purposes of a malicious prosecution claim. Defendant relies on Brennan v. Tremco Inc. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 310 for the proposition that arbitration decisions cannot be the basis for a malicious prosecution action. Plaintiff fails to address this decision, which is dispositive. Continues in Part 2 of 2...
